mainTopic: (continues) קניית כפל בשומר חנם · דין אמר הריני משלם
overview: (1) The Gemara analyzes why the Mishnah mentions both animals and utensils, and discusses how a bailee acquires the right to double payment from a thief before the theft occurs. (2) The Gemara discusses whether saying 'I will pay' is sufficient to acquire the right to double payment, analyzing the difference between a borrower and a bailee.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Gemara analyzes why the Mishnah mentions both animals and utensils, and discusses how a bailee acquires the right to double payment from a thief before the theft occurs.
The Gemara discusses whether saying 'I will pay' is sufficient to acquire the right to double payment, analyzing the difference between a borrower and a bailee.

steps.title: Rava's resolution: the transfer is conditional on the bailee's desire to pay
steps.stepSummary: Rava resolves the difficulty by framing the transfer as a conditional statement: when the bailee wants to pay, the rights are transferred from that moment.
steps.whatsHappening: Rava provides a novel mechanism to validate the transfer. This is a resolution step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Rava suggests the owner is deemed to have said: 'When it is stolen and you want to pay me, behold my cow is ceded to you from now.' This frames the transfer as taking effect at the moment of payment, not at the time of deposit.

keyTerms.meaning: he is deemed to have said to him
when it is stolen and you want to pay me

steps.whatToRemember: The transfer is conditional: 'לכשתגנב ותרצה ותשלמני'.
steps.confusionAlert: The phrase 'הרי פרתי קנויה לך מעכשיו' means the transfer takes effect retroactively from the moment of payment.
steps.whyThisMatters: This introduces the concept of conditional transfer to solve the דבר שלא בא לעולם problem.

phrases.english: Who is there to say that it will be stolen?
And if you find to say that it will be stolen –
Who is there to say that the thief will be found?
And if the thief is found –
Who is there to say that he will pay?
Perhaps he will admit and be exempt? Said Rava:
He becomes like one who said to him,

phrases.aramaic: לִכְשֶׁתִּגָּנֵב וְתִרְצֶה וּתְשַׁלְּמֵנִי הֲרֵי פָּרָתִי קְנוּיָה לְךָ מֵעַכְשָׁיו.
phrases.english: When it is stolen and you will want to pay me, behold my cow is ceded to you from now.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רָבָא: נַעֲשָׂה כְּאוֹמֵר לוֹ, לִכְשֶׁתִּגָּנֵב וְתִרְצֶה וּתְשַׁלְּמֵנִי הֲרֵי פָרָתִי קְנוּיָה לְךָ מֵעַכְשָׁיו.

rashi.hebrew: מי יימר דמגנבא - שעתיד להיגנב דליקני ליה כפל בשעת מסירה דעל כרחך משעת מסירה בעי לאקנויה שמשכה ממנו על מנת כן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the concern is whether the cow will actually be stolen, because the acquisition of the double payment must take effect at the time of the initial handover, when the bailee already performed a valid קניין.

rashi.hebrew: נעשה כאומר לו - בשעה שמסרה לו דקים להו לרבנן דניחא להו לבעלים שיהא בטוח בקרן על מנת שיהא ספק כפל העתיד לבא של שומר והרי היא כמסרה לו על מנת כן שאם תיגנב וישלם לו קרן שתהא פרה קנויה לו משעה שמסרה נמצא למפרע כשגנב הגנב של שומר היתה דפרה כבר היא בעולם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבנן assume the owner is happy to secure the principal, so the transfer is deemed conditional on future theft and payment, making the cow retroactively considered 'in the world' at the time of the theft.

tosafot.hebrew: דלמא מודה ומפטר - פי' דלמא ימהר להודות קודם שיבאו עדים והיינו כרב דאמר מודה בקנס ואח"כ באו עדים פטור ואע"ג דממתני' דמרובה (ב"ק דף עה:) דייקי' דלא כרב על פי עצמו דומיא דעל פי עד אחד לפי מסקנא דהתם דאמר מסתברא מלתיה דרב בשחייב עצמו בקרן אבל היכא דפטר עצמו מכלום לא אמר לא מפקא מדרב וכן הלכה ולא כשמואל דמחייב דר' יוחנן סבר נמי התם דפטור ושמואל ור' יוחנן הלכה כר' יוחנן כדאמר פ' מי שהוציאוהו (עירובין דף מז:):
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the concern 'perhaps he will admit and be exempt' refers to the possibility that the bailee will confess before witnesses arrive, following רב's ruling that such an admission exempts from the fine, which is the הלכה.

steps.title: Rav Zera's objection: if so, why exclude fleece and offspring?
steps.stepSummary: Rav Zera challenges Rava's resolution by pointing out that it would also apply to the animal's fleece and offspring, which the Mishna explicitly excludes.
steps.whatsHappening: Rav Zera attacks Rava's resolution with a counter-example from the Mishna. This is an objection step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If the transfer is conditional on the bailee's desire to pay, then the owner should also be transferring rights to the fleece and offspring, which are also future benefits. But the Mishna explicitly excludes them.
steps.whatToRemember: The Mishna excludes גיזותיה וולדותיה (fleece and offspring).
steps.confusionAlert: This is a classic 'אי הכי' objection: if your resolution is correct, then X should also apply, but it doesn't.
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces a refinement of Rava's resolution.

phrases.aramaic: מַתְקֵיף לַהּ רַבִּי זֵירָא: אִי הָכִי,
phrases.english: Rav Zera strengthened against it: If so,
even its fleece and its offspring as well!
What is taught: except for its fleece and its offspring?
Rather, said Rav Zera:
He becomes like one who said to him,
except for its fleece and its offspring.

steps.triggerLanguage: מַתְקֵיף לַהּ רַבִּי זֵירָא: אִי הָכִי, אֲפִילּוּ גִּזּוֹתֶיהָ וּוַלְדוֹתֶיהָ נָמֵי!

rashi.hebrew: אי הכי - דנמצא שהיתה שלו משעה ראשונה יזכה אף בגיזות וולדות שהיו לה משבאה לביתו דכיון דנגנב ורצה ושילם נמצאת שלו למפרע:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that if the owner's rights are retroactive from the moment of theft, he should automatically acquire the fleece and offspring as well, making the Mishna's explicit exclusion of them puzzling.

steps.title: Why is the Mishna's ruling unqualified?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks why the Mishna rules without qualification, given the need to exclude fleece and offspring.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara asks a neutral question about the Mishna's wording. This is a setup/transition line.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Mishna doesn't explicitly exclude fleece and offspring. The Gemara asks why the ruling is unqualified, given that the exclusion is necessary.

keyTerms.meaning: and why is it a decision
the unqualified way of the matter

steps.confusionAlert: This is a neutral question, not an objection.
steps.whyThisMatters: This leads to a general principle about what is typically included in such transfers.

phrases.english: And what is the decision?

phrases.aramaic: סְתָמָא דְּמִלְּתָא שְׁבָחָא דְּאָתֵא מֵעָלְמָא, עֲבִיד אִינִישׁ דְּמַקְנֵי.
phrases.english: The unqualified way of the matter, the gain that comes from the world, a person does to cede.

phrases.aramaic: שְׁבָחָא דְּמִגּוּפַהּ, לָא עֲבִיד אִינִישׁ דְּמַקְנֵי.
phrases.english: The gain that comes from itself, a person does not do to cede.

rashi.hebrew: פסקא - פסקת הדבר בכל אדם שדעתו להקנות כפל אם יבא לכך ואין דעתו להקנות הולדות:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's question of why the Mishna is unqualified (פסקא) is answered by the principle that people generally intend to transfer ownership of external improvements (שבחא דאתא מעלמא) but not of intrinsic growth (שבחא דמגופה), such as offspring.

steps.title: Alternative version of רַבָּא's ruling: transfer takes effect adjacent to the theft
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara presents an alternative version of Rava's ruling, where the transfer takes effect adjacent to the theft, not at the moment of payment.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces an alternative version of רַבָּא's ruling. This is a setup line presenting a different textual tradition.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Some say רַבָּא ruled: 'When it is stolen and you want to pay me, adjacent to the theft, it is ceded to you.' This frames the transfer as taking effect at the time of the theft, not at the moment of payment. This avoids the דָּבָר שֶׁלֹּא בָּא לָעוֹלָם problem by tying the transfer to the theft event.

phrases.english: There are those who say, said Rava:
He becomes as one who said to him,
When it is stolen and you will want to pay me,

phrases.aramaic: סָמוּךְ לִגְנֵיבָתָהּ קְנוּיָה לָךְ. מַאי בֵּינַיְיהוּ?
phrases.english: Adjacent to its theft it is acquired for you. What between them?

steps.triggerLanguage: אִיכָּא דְּאָמְרִי, אָמַר רָבָא: נַעֲשָׂה כְּאוֹמֵר לוֹ, לִכְשֶׁתִּגָּנֵב וְתִרְצֶה וּתְשַׁלְּמֵנִי, סָמוּךְ לִגְנֵיבָתָהּ קְנוּיָה לָךְ.

rashi.hebrew: נעשה כאומר לו - בשעה שמסרה לו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase נעשה כאומר לו refers to the moment the owner hands the animal over to the bailee.

rashi.hebrew: לכשתגנב ותרצה ותשלמני - אם תשלם לי הקרן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the condition לכשתגנב ותרצה ותשלמני means if the owner pays the principal amount.

steps.title: The difference is רַבִּי זֵירָא's objection and the case of an animal in a marsh
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara answers that the difference is Rav Zera's objection and the case of an animal standing in a marsh.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara provides an answer to the previous question. This is a direct resolution step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara answers: 'There is between them רַבִּי זֵירָא's objection. Or also, when it is standing in a marsh.' רַבִּי זֵירָא's objection applies to the first version but not the second. The case of an animal in a marsh is another practical difference.

keyTerms.meaning: when it is standing in a marsh

phrases.english: There is between them a difficulty of Rabbi Zera.
If also it is standing in a marsh.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִיכָּא בֵּינַיְיהוּ קוּשְׁיָא דְּרַבִּי זֵירָא. אִי נָמֵי דְּקַיְּמָא בַּאֲגָם.

rashi.hebrew: קושיא דר' זירא - ללישנא בתרא ליכא לאקשויי גיזות וולדות דהא סמוך לגניבתה הוא דאקנייה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that according to the second version (לישנא בתרא), רבי זירא's objection regarding גיזות וולדות does not apply because the acquisition occurs immediately adjacent to the theft (סמוך לגניבתה).

rashi.hebrew: א"נ דהוה קיימא באגם - כשגנבה גנב ללישנא בתרא לא קני כפילא דהא סמוך לגניבתה לא היתה בחצירו שתהא חצירו קונה לו ומשיכה ההוא שעתה לא הואי ובמאי נקני וא"ת תקני לו משיכה ראשונה שמשכה לו על מנת לקנותה שעה הסמוכה לגניבתה הא אמרינן בכתובות בהאשה שנפלו לה נכסים (דף פב.) האומר לחבירו משוך פרה זו ולא תיקני לך אלא לאחר שלשים לא קנה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that in the case of an animal in a marsh (אגם), the second version does not acquire the offspring (כפילא) because the animal was not in the thief's courtyard at the time of the initial transfer, and a delayed acquisition (משיכה ראשונה שמשכה לו על מנת לקנותה שעה הסמוכה לגניבתה) is invalid based on the principle in כתובות that one cannot acquire something conditionally for a future time.

tosafot.hebrew: אי נמי דקיימא באגם - תימה דנימא נעשה כאמר לו לכשתגנב ותשלמני הרי פרתי קנויה לך מעכשיו סמוך לגניבה דהשתא קני אפילו עומדת באגם כדאמר ר' יוחנן בפ' האשה שנפלו (כתובות ד' פב. ושם) משוך פרה זו ותקנה לך מעכשיו ולאחר שלשים יום קנה אפי' עומדת באגם וגיזותיה וולדותיה לא קנה אע"פ שאומר מעכשיו שאין הקנין נגמר עד סמוך לגניבתה כדאמר ר' יוחנן בפ' האומר (קידושין דף ס.) בא אחד וקדשה מעכשיו ולאחר שלשים יום בא אחר וקדשה מעכשיו ולאחר כ' יום בא אחר וקדשה מעכשיו ולאחר עשרה ימים בא אחר וקדשה מעכשיו אפי' הן מאה כולם תופסים בה דכל חד וחד רווחא לחבריה שבק ושמא י"ל דקדושין אלימי וכיון דאשכחו רווחא תו לא פקעי אבל הכא גיזות וולדות לכל הפחות הנהו דאיתנהו בשעת מסירה אהני מעכשיו לקנותה סמוך לגניבתה למפרע:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות questions why we cannot apply the principle from כתובות that a delayed acquisition (מעכשיו ולאחר שלשים) is valid even for an animal in a marsh, and suggests that perhaps קידושין are stronger, or that the initial transfer only acquires the animal itself at the time of transfer, not the future גיזות וולדות.

meforshim.interplaySummary: רש״י and תוספות both address the validity of acquisition in the case of an animal in a marsh, but תוספות raises a difficulty based on a ruling in כתובות that רש״י uses to invalidate the acquisition, questioning why that ruling doesn't apply here.

steps.title: רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן: הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם = שִׁלּוּם מַמָּשׁ
steps.stepSummary: רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן defines the Mishna's term 'שִׁלֵּם' as including the verbal declaration 'הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם', even without actual payment.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with a מִימְרָא from רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן interpreting the מִשְׁנָה's language. This is a setup line introducing the core principle.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The מִשְׁנָה discusses a case where a bailee 'שִׁלֵּם' (paid) but refused to swear. רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן clarifies that 'שִׁלֵּם' does not require actual monetary transfer; the verbal commitment 'הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם' suffices to transfer the right to כֶּפֶל from the thief to the bailee.

phrases.english: He paid and did not want to swear [etc.].

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַבִּי חִיָּיא בַּר אַבָּא אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן:
phrases.english: Said Rabbi Chiya bar Abba, said Rabbi Yochanan:
Not 'paid' — paid truly,
but rather since he said 'Behold, I am paying',
even though he did not pay.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַבִּי חִיָּיא בַּר אַבָּא אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן: לֹא ״שִׁלֵּם״ – שִׁלֵּם מַמָּשׁ, אֶלָּא כֵיוָן שֶׁאָמַר ״הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם״, אַף עַל פִּי שֶׁלֹּא שִׁלֵּם.

steps.title: Challenge from the מִשְׁנָה's explicit wording
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's reading by pointing out that the Mishna explicitly contrasts 'שִׁלֵּם' with 'לֹא שִׁלֵּם', implying actual payment is required.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara raises a קוֹשְׁיָא against רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן based on the מִשְׁנָה's own text. This is a challenge step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The מִשְׁנָה states 'שִׁלֵּם – אִין, לֹא שִׁלֵּם – לָא'. This explicit contrast suggests that actual payment is the dividing line. The Gemara also notes that the latter part of the מִשְׁנָה ('נִשְׁבַּע וְלֹא רָצָה לְשַׁלֵּם') implies that willingness to pay might suffice, but concludes that this cannot be derived from the מִשְׁנָה ('מֵהָא לֵיכָא לְמִשְׁמַע מִינָהּ').

phrases.english: We learned: He paid and did not want to swear.
Paid – yes, did not pay – no.

phrases.aramaic: אֵימָא סֵיפָא: נִשְׁבַּע וְלֹא רָצָה לְשַׁלֵּם.
phrases.english: Let us say the latter part: He swore and did not want to pay.
The reason is that he did not want [to pay],

phrases.aramaic: הָא רָצָה אַף עַל פִּי שֶׁלֹּא שִׁילֵּם.
phrases.english: If he wanted, even though he did not pay.
Rather from here we cannot derive anything from it.

steps.triggerLanguage: תַּנְנָן: שִׁלֵּם וְלֹא רָצָה לִשָּׁבַע. שִׁלֵּם – אִין, לֹא שִׁלֵּם – לָא.

tosafot.hebrew: אלא מהא ליכא למשמע מינה - תימה כיון דקשיא ליה רישא לסיפא נימא נעשה כדאמרינן בפרק קמא דקידושין (דף ה:) ותקשה לר' יוחנן וי"ל דשילם נקט לאביי דפרק הגוזל קמא (ב"ק ד' קח.) לאורויי דאם נשבע ושילם דאין הכפל שלו ולרבא לאורויי שילם אע"פ שנשבע תחלה שהכפל שלו ולא רצה לישבע היינו לא רצה לעמוד בשבועה אבל אם אמר הריני משלם אם נשבע תחלה אין הכפל שלו:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the Gemara does not simply resolve the contradiction by stating 'נעשה' (we will assume the Mishna is referring to a different case) because the term שילם is used to clarify the law according to אביי and רבא regarding whether the double payment belongs to the thief when he pays after swearing.

steps.title: בְּרֵיתָא supporting רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן
steps.stepSummary: A ברייתא is cited that explicitly supports רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן: a renter who says 'הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם' acquires the right to כֶּפֶל.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara brings a בְּרֵיתָא to resolve the קוֹשְׁיָא and support רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן. This is a proof step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The בְּרֵיתָא describes a case where a renter (שׂוֹכֵר) says 'הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם וְאֵינִי נִשְׁבָּע' and the thief is later found. The thief pays כֶּפֶל to the renter. This proves that verbal commitment alone transfers the right to כֶּפֶל, validating רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's reading of the מִשְׁנָה.

phrases.english: It was taught in a baraita like Rabbi Yochanan:
One who rents a cow from his fellow,
and it was stolen, and he said:
"Behold, I am paying and I do not swear"
and afterwards the thief was found –
he pays double payment to the renter.

steps.triggerLanguage: תַּנְיָא כְּוָתֵיהּ דְּרַבִּי יוֹחָנָן: הַשּׂוֹכֵר פָּרָה מֵחֲבֵרוֹ, וְנִגְנְבָה, וְאָמַר הָלָה: ״הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם וְאֵינִי נִשְׁבָּע״, וְאַחַר כָּךְ נִמְצָא הַגַּנָּב – מְשַׁלֵּם תַּשְׁלוּמֵי כֶפֶל לַשּׂוֹכֵר.

rashi.hebrew: השוכר פרה ונגנבה כו' - אע"פ שהשוכר חייב בגניבה ואבידה מ"מ אם רוצה לשקר ולישבע שנאנסה היה נפטר בשבועה הלכך כי אמר נגנבה וחייב עצמו בקרן נקנה לו הכפל:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that although the שוכר is liable for גניבה, he could have been exempted by a שבועה claiming אונס, so his verbal commitment to pay the קרן effectively acquires the כפל for him.

steps.title: רַב פַּפָּא: שׁוֹמֵר חִנָּם and שׁוֹמֵר שְׂכָר acquire כֶּפֶל by speech
steps.stepSummary: רַב פַּפָּא extends the principle to שׁוֹמֵר חִנָּם and שׁוֹמֵר שְׂכָר: their verbal admission transfers the right to כֶּפֶל because they could have claimed exemption.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב פַּפָּא expands the rule to other types of bailees, explaining the underlying logic. This is a ruling step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב פַּפָּא rules that a שׁוֹמֵר חִנָּם who says 'פָּשַׁעְתִּי' (I was negligent) and a שׁוֹמֵר שְׂכָר who says 'נִגְנְבָה' (it was stolen) acquire the right to כֶּפֶל. The logic is that each bailee could have claimed exemption (שׁוֹמֵר חִנָּם from theft, שׁוֹמֵר שְׂכָר from breakage/death). By admitting liability, they waive that exemption and transfer the right to כֶּפֶל.

phrases.english: Said Rav Pappa: An unpaid guardian,
Since he said "I was negligent",
He cedes to him the double payment.
For if he wanted, he could exempt himself with theft.

phrases.aramaic: שׁוֹמֵר שָׂכָר, כֵּיוָן שֶׁאָמַר ״נִגְנְבָה״, מַקְנֵה לֵיהּ כְּפֵילָא.
phrases.english: A paid guardian, since he said "It was stolen", he cedes to him the double payment.

phrases.aramaic: דְּאִי בָּעֵי פָּטַר נַפְשֵׁיהּ בִּשְׁבוּרָה וּמֵתָה.
phrases.english: For if he wanted, he could exempt himself with broken or died.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַב פַּפָּא: שׁוֹמֵר חִנָּם, כֵיוָן שֶׁאָמַר ״פָּשַׁעְתִּי״, מַקְנֵה לֵיהּ כְּפֵילָא. דְּאִי בָעֵי פָטַר נַפְשֵׁיהּ בִּגְנֵבָה. שׁוֹמֵר שְׂכָר, כֵיוָן שֶׁאָמַר ״נִגְנְבָה״, מַקְנֵה לֵיהּ כְּפֵילָא. דְּאִי בָעֵי פָטַר נַפְשֵׁיהּ בִּשְׁבוּרָה וּמֵתָה.

rashi.hebrew: מקנה ליה כפילא - נקנה לו הכפל: כל חיובי ופטורי דארבעה שומרין ילפינן מקראי לקמן בפרק השואל (בבא מציעא דף צד:): שומר חנם ישבע על הכל אם לא פשע והשואל משלם את הכל נושא שכר והשוכר פטורין מן האונסין וחייבין בגניבה ואבידה שואל פטור במתה מחמת מלאכה הראויה לה דאמר ליה להכי אושלת לי ולא לאוקמה בכילתא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the liability and exemption rules for the four types of bailees are derived from verses, as detailed in פרק השואל, and clarifies that a שׁוֹמֵר חִנָּם is exempt from אונסין unless negligent, while a שׁוֹאֵל is liable for everything except death due to normal work.

steps.title: רַב פַּפָּא: שׁוֹאֵל does NOT acquire כֶּפֶל by speech
steps.stepSummary: רַב פַּפָּא rules that a שׁוֹאֵל (borrower) does NOT acquire כֶּפֶל by saying 'הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם' because he has no exemption to waive.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב פַּפָּא distinguishes the שׁוֹאֵל from the other bailees. This is a ruling step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב פַּפָּא rules that a שׁוֹאֵל who says 'הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם' does NOT acquire the right to כֶּפֶל. The reason is that a שׁוֹאֵל is liable for almost everything, including breakage and death. The only potential exemption is 'מֵתָה מֵחֲמַת מְלָאכָה' (died due to work), but this is rare ('לָא שְׁכִיחַ'). Since he has no meaningful exemption to waive, his speech does not transfer the right.

phrases.english: A borrower who says, 'Behold I will pay,'
does not cede him the double payment,
With what could he have exempted himself?

phrases.aramaic: בְּמֵתָה מֵחֲמַת מְלָאכָה, מֵתָה מֵחֲמַת מְלָאכָה לָא שְׁכִיחַ.
phrases.english: With [a claim of] it died on account of work? 'It died on account of work' [however] is not common.

steps.triggerLanguage: שׁוֹאֵל שֶׁאוֹמֵר ״הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם״ – לָא מַקְנֵי לֵיהּ כְּפֵילָא, בְּמַאי הָוָה לֵיהּ לְמִפְטַר נַפְשֵׁיהּ? בְּמֵתָה מֵחֲמַת מְלָאכָה, מֵתָה מֵחֲמַת מְלָאכָה לָא שְׁכִיחַ.

steps.title: Alternative view: שׁוֹאֵל DOES acquire כֶּפֶל by speech
steps.stepSummary: An alternative view attributes to רַב פַּפָּא the opinion that a שׁוֹאֵל DOES acquire כֶּפֶל by speech, even if the exemption is rare.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara presents a second version of רַב פַּפָּא's opinion. This is a setup line introducing a contradiction.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Some say רַב פַּפָּא held that a שׁוֹאֵל DOES acquire כֶּפֶל by saying 'הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם'. The logic is that even though 'מֵתָה מֵחֲמַת מְלָאכָה' is rare, it is still a theoretical exemption he could have claimed. By saying 'הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם', he waives even this rare exemption.

phrases.english: There are those who say, Rav Pappa said:
A borrower also, since he said "Behold I will pay" –
he cedes to him the double payment,

phrases.aramaic: דְּאִי בָּעֵי פָּטַר נַפְשֵׁיהּ בְּמֵתָה מֵחֲמַת מְלָאכָה.
phrases.english: for if he wanted, he could have exempted himself with it died due to work.
Rav Zevid said to him:
Thus said Abaye:
A borrower until he pays. What is the reason?
Since all the benefit is his,
with speech he does not cede to him the double payment.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִיכָּא דְּאָמְרִי, אָמַר רַב פַּפָּא: שׁוֹאֵל נָמֵי כֵיוָן שֶׁאָמַר ״הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם״ – מַקְנֵי לֵיהּ כְּפֵילָא, דְּאִי בָעֵי פָטַר נַפְשֵׁיהּ בְּמֵתָה מֵחֲמַת מְלָאכָה.

rashi.hebrew: וכל הנאה שלו - שהיה עושה בה מלאכתו בלא שום שכר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase 'וְכָל הֲנָאָה שֶׁלּוֹ' refers to the borrower deriving benefit from the item by using it for his work without paying any wages.
רש״י clarifies that 'בְּדִבּוּרָא' specifically refers to the borrower's declaration of 'הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם'.

rashi.hebrew: לא מקני ליה - הבעלים כפל שאין זו שום טובה מוטל היה עליו להניח דעתו שהשאילה לו חנם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the owner does not acquire double payment through speech because the borrower was already obligated to pay for the item's use, so the declaration provides no new benefit to the owner.

tosafot.hebrew: שואל עד שישלם - הואיל וכל הנאה שלו ולהאי טעמא אפי' אם התנה להיות כש"ח לא קנה עד שישלם ולטעמא דלעיל דמשום לא שכיחא אם התנה להיות כש"ח קנה כפל במגו דפטר נפשיה בגניבה ואבידה דשכיחא:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that according to אַבָּיֵי's reasoning that the borrower derives all benefit, even if the borrower stipulated to be treated like a שׁוֹאֵל, he would not acquire double payment until he actually pays; however, according to the earlier reasoning regarding the rarity of 'מֵתָה מֵחֲמַת מְלָאכָה', such a stipulation would allow him to acquire double payment because he waives the common exemptions of theft and loss.

meforshim.interplaySummary: תוספות contrasts the two underlying rationales for the borrower's liability, showing how each rationale leads to a different halachic outcome regarding the effect of a stipulation to be treated like a שׁוֹאֵל.

steps.title: בְּרֵיתָא supporting אַבָּיֵי
steps.stepSummary: A ברייתא is cited that supports אַבָּיֵי: a שׁוֹאֵל who actually pays acquires the right to כֶּפֶל.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא brings a בְּרֵיתָא to support אַבָּיֵי's view. This is a setup line introducing a new source.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The בְּרֵיתָא describes a שׁוֹאֵל who borrows a cow, it is stolen, and he 'קִדֵּם וְשִׁלֵּם'. When the thief is found, the thief pays כֶּפֶל to the שׁוֹאֵל. This implies actual payment is required for a שׁוֹאֵל to acquire the right, supporting אַבָּיֵי.
steps.whatToRemember: The בְּרֵיתָא explicitly mentions 'שִׁלֵּם' for a שׁוֹאֵל.

phrases.english: It was taught like Rav Zevid:
One who borrows a cow from his fellow and it is stolen,
and the borrower hastens and pays,
and afterwards the thief is found –
he pays double payment to the borrower.

steps.triggerLanguage: תַּנְיָא כְּווֹתֵיהּ דְּרַב זְבִיד: הַשּׁוֹאֵל פָּרָה מֵחֲבֵרוֹ וְנִגְנְבָה, וְקִדֵּם הַשּׁוֹאֵל וְשִׁלֵּם, וְאַחַר כָּךְ נִמְצָא הַגַּנָּב – מְשַׁלֵּם תַּשְׁלוּמֵי כֶפֶל לַשּׁוֹאֵל.

steps.title: Is the בְּרֵיתָא a refutation of רַב פַּפָּא's second view?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara questions whether the ברייתא refutes רַב פַּפָּא's second view (that שׁוֹאֵל acquires by speech).
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא analyzes the relationship between the בְּרֵיתָא and רַב פַּפָּא's views. This is a transition line setting up a challenge.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The בְּרֵיתָא is clearly not a refutation of רַב פַּפָּא's first view. But for the second view, the בְּרֵיתָא's emphasis on actual payment seems to contradict it. The גמרא asks if this is a תְּיוּבְתָּא.
steps.whatToRemember: The בְּרֵיתָא's wording is the key to the refutation.
steps.confusionAlert: The גמרא distinguishes between the two views of רַב פַּפָּא.

phrases.english: For the first language of Rav Pappa
Certainly it was not a refutation.
For the second language, shall we say it is his refutation?

steps.triggerLanguage: לְלִישָׁנָא קַמָּא דְּרַב פַּפָּא – וַדַּאי לָא הָוְיָא תְּיוּבְתָּא. לְלִישָׁנָא בָּתְרָא לֵימָא תֵּיהְוֵי תְּיוּבְתֵּיהּ?

rashi.hebrew: ללישנא קמא דר"פ - ודאי לא הוי תיובתא דנימא כיון דטעמא דרב פפא משום דלא הוה למפטר נפשיה דהא מתה מחמת מלאכה לא שכיחא אפי' קדם ושילם נמי אית ליה לרב פפא דלא קני ליה כפילא ותיקשי ליה הא ודאי ליכא לאותובי דמצי אמר לך אנא אע"פ שאמר הריני משלם אמרי ולא אמרי אע"פ ששילם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the ברייתא is not a refutation of רַב פַּפָּא's first view because רַב פַּפָּא can argue that even if the borrower actually paid, he still did not acquire the double payment, since the reason for exemption is that death from work is rare.

rashi.hebrew: נימא תיהוי תיובתא - מי נימא דשילם דוקא קאמר תנא דברייתא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the Gemara's question about a refutation for the second view hinges on whether the תנא of the ברייתא specifically requires actual payment ('שִׁלֵּם דוקא') rather than just a declaration of intent to pay.

tosafot.hebrew: ללישנא קמא דרב פפא ודאי לא הויא תיובתא - דאמר הריני משלם לא קנה כפל אבל שילם קנה כפל ור"ח גרס ודאי הויא תיובתא דכיון דרב פפא מפרש טעמא משום דלא שכיח האי טעמא שייך אפילו בשילם והא דנקט ואמר הריני משלם משום דנקט גבי ש"ח וש"ש נקט נמי גבי שואל:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the ברייתa is not a refutation of the first view because רַב פַּפָּא holds that a declaration ('הריני משלם') does not confer acquisition, but actual payment ('שילם') does, whereas ר

meforshim.interplaySummary: תוספות presents a different understanding of רַב פַּפָּא's first view than רש״י, arguing that actual payment does confer acquisition in the first view, and notes ר

steps.title: רַב פַּפָּא: The בְּרֵיתָא also means 'said'
steps.stepSummary: רַב פַּפָּא resolves the difficulty by arguing that the ברייתa's 'שִׁלֵּם' also means 'said', just like the Mishna.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב פַּפָּא defends his second view against the בְּרֵיתָא. This is a resolution attempt.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב פַּפָּא argues that the בְּרֵיתָא is not stronger than the מִשְׁנָה. The מִשְׁנָה says 'שִׁלֵּם' and we interpreted it as 'said'. The בְּרֵיתָא also says 'שִׁלֵּם', so it too should be interpreted as 'said'.
steps.whatToRemember: רַב פַּפָּא equates the בְּרֵיתָא's wording with the מִשְׁנָה's.
steps.confusionAlert: This relies on the earlier resolution of the מִשְׁנָה.
steps.whyThisMatters: Attempts to preserve רַב פַּפָּא's second view.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר לְךָ רַב פָּפָּא: מִי אַלִּימָא מִמַּתְנִיתִין?
phrases.english: Rav Pappa said to you: Is it stronger than our Mishnah?

phrases.aramaic: דְּקָתָנֵי ״שִׁילֵּם״, וְאוֹקֵימְנָא, בְּאָמַר הָכָא נָמֵי בְּאָמַר.
phrases.english: It teaches 'he paid', and we established it as 'he said'. Here too, it is 'he said'.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר לְךָ רַב פַּפָּא: מִי אַלִּימָא מִמַּתְנִיתִין? דְּקָתָנֵי ״שִׁלֵּם״, וְאוֹקֵימְנָא, בְּאָמַר הָכָא נָמֵי בְּאָמַר.

steps.title: The בְּרֵיתָא uses 'קִדֵּם' unlike the מִשְׁנָה
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges רַב פַּפָּא's resolution by pointing out that the ברייתa uses the term 'קִדֵּם' (hastened), which the Mishna does not.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a new difficulty against רַב פַּפָּא's resolution. This is a challenge line.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The מִשְׁנָה does not use the term 'קִדֵּם', but the בְּרֵיתָא does. This suggests that 'קִדֵּם' adds something. The גמרא asks what 'קִדֵּם' means, implying it might mean 'hastened and said' or something else.
steps.whatToRemember: The term 'קִדֵּם' is the new difficulty.
steps.confusionAlert: The גמרא is probing the meaning of 'קִדֵּם'.

phrases.english: Is it similar?
There it was not learned "he hastened," here it was learned "he hastened"!
What is "he hastened"? He hastened and said.

steps.triggerLanguage: מִי דָּמֵי? הָתָם לָא קָתָנֵי ״קִדֵּם״, הָכָא קָתָנֵי ״קִדֵּם״! מַאי ״קִדֵּם״? קִדֵּם וְאָמַר.

steps.title: The בְּרֵיתָא's wording implies precision
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara argues that the ברייתa's different wording for שׂוֹכֵר ('וְאָמַר') and שׁוֹאֵל ('קִדֵּם') implies that 'קִדֵּם' means actual payment, not just speech.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא presses the difficulty by analyzing the בְּרֵיתָא's precise wording. This is a challenge line.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The בְּרֵיתָא uses 'וְאָמַר' for the שׂוֹכֵר and 'קִדֵּם' for the שׁוֹאֵל. This difference in wording suggests that the תַנָּא was precise. If 'קִדֵּם' meant 'hastened and said', why not use 'וְאָמַר' for both?
steps.whatToRemember: The difference in wording is key.
steps.confusionAlert: The גמרא is using a דִּקְדּוּק argument.
steps.whyThisMatters: Strengthens the refutation of רַב פַּפָּא's second view.

phrases.english: Behold, it learned concerning the renter, "and he said,"
and concerning the borrower, "he hastened" –
We infer from it that it taught precisely!
Were [the two statements] learned together?

steps.triggerLanguage: הָא מִדְּקָתָנֵי גַבֵּי שׂוֹכֵר ״וְאָמַר״, וְגַבֵּי שׁוֹאֵל ״קִדֵּם״ – שְׁמַע מִינָהּ דַּוְקָא קָתָנֵי!

rashi.hebrew: והא מדקתני - תנא דברייתא גבי שוכר ואמר כדאמרינן לעיל תניא כוותיה דר' יוחנן וגבי שואל ושילם בהך דתניא כוותיה דרב זביד:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the ברייתa's precise wording of 'וְאָמַר' for the שׂוֹכֵר and 'שִׁלֵּם' for the שׁוֹאֵל reflects the respective opinions of רַב יוֹחָנָן and רַב זְבִיד.

steps.title: Were the two statements learned together?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks whether the two statements in the ברייתa (about שׂוֹכֵר and שׁוֹאֵל) were learned together, which would validate the דִּקְדּוּק argument.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a technical question about the בְּרֵיתָא's structure. This is a setup line for a technical point.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The דִּקְדּוּק argument only works if the two statements were learned together. If they were learned separately, the difference in wording might be coincidental. The גמרא asks the experts and they confirm that the statements were learned together.
steps.whatToRemember: The confirmation validates the דִּקְדּוּק argument.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a technical question about Tannaitic compilation.
steps.whyThisMatters: Establishes the validity of the refutation.

phrases.aramaic: שַׁיְילִינְהוּ לְתַנָּאֵי דְּבֵי רַבִּי חִיָּיא וּדְבֵי רַבִּי אוֹשַׁעְיָא,
phrases.english: We ask the teachers of the House of Rabbi Chiya and the House of Rabbi Oshaya,
and they say: Together we learned them.

steps.triggerLanguage: מִידֵי גַבֵּי הֲדָדֵי תַנְיָא? שַׁיְילִינְהוּ לְתַנָּאֵי דְּבֵי רַבִּי חִיָּיא וּדְבֵי רַבִּי אוֹשַׁעְיָא, וְאָמְרִי: גַבֵּי הֲדָדֵי תַנְיָין.

rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the term תַנָּאֵי refers to the students of the ישיבה who were asked this question.

rashi.hebrew: לתנאי - שהיו שונין תוספתא דרבי חייא ותוספתא דרבי אושעיא שהם עיקר כדאמרינן בעלמא (חולין דף קמא.): כל מתניתא דלא מיתניא בי רבי אושעיא ורבי חייא לא תותבוה בי מדרשא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the תַנָּאֵי דְּבֵי רַבִּי חִיָּיא וּדְבֵי רַבִּי אוֹשַׁעְיָא are the authorities on the תוספתא, citing the principle that any מיתא not learned in their academies is not accepted in the בי מדרשא.

steps.title: The sugya concludes with a new case
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara moves to a new case: what if the bailee first says 'I will not pay' and then says 'I will pay'?
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא transitions to a new scenario. This is a transition line.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא notes that if a bailee first says 'אֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם' and then says 'הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם', it is obvious that he acquires the right to כֶּפֶל. The גמרא then begins to discuss the opposite case: what if he first says 'הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם' and then says 'אֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם'?
steps.confusionAlert: The sugya is cut off mid-sentence.

phrases.aramaic: פְּשִׁיטָא: אָמַר ״אֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם״ וְחָזַר וְאָמַר ״הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם״,
phrases.english: It is obvious: he said "I will not pay" and returned and said "Behold, I will pay",
– behold, he said "Behold, I will pay".
But he said "Behold, I will pay"

steps.triggerLanguage: פְּשִׁיטָא: אָמַר ״אֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם״ וְחָזַר וְאָמַר ״הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם״, – הָא קָאָמַר ״הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם״. אֶלָּא אָמַר ״הֲרֵינִי מְשַׁלֵּם״
